critic_raw:
The methodology is carefully constructed and avoids most of the common failure modes in security ML experiments. The temporal split is correct, the campaign-coherence constraint on splits is explicitly stated and well-motivated, the baseline choice is defensible, and the scope is honestly bounded. That said, I identify three issues worth examining — one is a genuine confound, one is a prerequisite assumption that may not hold, and one is a measurement design choice that could inflate the apparent benefit of the GNN.

**Issue 1: The graph construction window creates an asymmetric information advantage for the GNN that is not attributable to relational inductive bias**

The claim being made: Any performance difference between the GNN and the GBDT is attributable to relational modeling — the ability to propagate information across users, hosts, IPs, and processes — rather than to richer or differently aggregated feature content.

Why this might be wrong: The graph is built from seven days of prior telemetry, structured as a heterogeneous graph over authentication sessions, processes, and network connections. The GBDT baseline uses host-day aggregates. Even with the ablation that removes graph edges, the node features in the GNN may still encode multi-entity temporal context (e.g., a process node attribute derived from its full activity history across all hosts it has appeared on) that is simply not present in the tabular host-day features. The ablation tests 'graph with edges removed' versus 'graph without edges,' not 'graph node features' versus 'tabular features.' If the GNN's node feature construction pipeline aggregates signals that cross host boundaries before the GNN even runs message passing, then the ablation cannot isolate relational inductive bias from richer cross-host feature engineering. The edge-removal ablation would then show a drop that is partly due to the node features already encoding relational information implicitly.

What would constitute evidence: Compare the tabular GBDT features explicitly against the GNN node features field-by-field. If the GNN node feature set includes any quantity that requires joining across more than one host or more than one user at featurization time, that quantity should either appear in the GBDT feature set too, or the experiment should report a second GBDT trained on the node features directly (i.e., GNN node feature vector as input to GBDT, no edges). If that second GBDT matches or beats the full GNN, the gain was in the features, not the structure.

**Issue 2: The seven-day positive labeling window interacts with the campaign-coherence split in a way that may silently contaminate the test set**

The claim being made: Campaign-coherence grouping by first observed malicious activity prevents later intrusion stages from appearing in evaluation while earlier stages are in training.

Why this might be wrong: The labeling rule marks a host-day positive if the host falls within seven days of a confirmed malicious interval. The campaign grouping is by first observed malicious activity in the ticket timeline. Consider a campaign that begins in month 10 (training) and whose forensic scoping determines it ran through month 13 (validation). Host-days in months 12-13 that are labeled positive under the seven-day window rule are assigned to training because the campaign's first observation is in month 10. This is correct under the stated rule. But now consider the converse: a campaign first observed in month 15 (test), whose forensic scoping reaches back and confirms malicious activity starting in month 14 (validation). Host-days in month 14 that fall within seven days of this campaign's confirmed interval should be positive, but because the campaign's first observation is month 15, those host-days might be sitting in the validation split with negative or excluded labels, depending on the exclusion logic for unresolved investigations. If the seven-day window and the first-observation grouping are not implemented with explicit bidirectional alignment checks — does every host-day in every split have a label consistent with the campaign assignment rule? — there will be mislabeled examples near split boundaries, with the direction of the mislabeling being that some validation/test positives are labeled negative, depressing measured recall for both models equally and masking whether either model is actually detecting these boundary cases.

What would constitute evidence: Audit the distribution of positive host-days relative to split boundaries. For each confirmed campaign, plot the host-day labels as a function of offset from the split boundary. If there is a systematic drop in positive rate in the final week before each split boundary, that is evidence of mislabeled boundary examples. The rate of excluded host-days near boundaries should also be reported; if it is large, it may be inadvertently filtering out exactly the early-detection cases that the hypothesis cares about.

**Issue 3: AUPRC computed on a single contiguous test window is sensitive to campaign clustering in a way that makes confidence intervals misleading**

The claim being made: AUPRC on months 16-18 is a reliable measure of whether the GNN surfaces intrusions earlier in the queue across future attack windows.

Why this might be wrong: AUPRC integrates over all operating points, but in a three-month test window, the number of distinct campaigns is small — likely single digits to low tens in a single enterprise environment. AUPRC is dominated by whether the model happens to score the campaigns present in that specific window highly. If two or three large campaigns account for the majority of positive host-days, the AUPRC estimate is essentially measuring performance on those two or three campaigns. A model that is structurally better in expectation could lose on this window due to one idiosyncratic campaign the GNN fails on, or win due to one campaign that happens to match GNN's inductive bias. The stated controls for campaign novelty help interpret results but do not fix the variance of the estimator. Bootstrap confidence intervals on AUPRC will understate true uncertainty because they resample host-days, not campaigns, and host-days within a campaign are correlated.

What would constitute evidence: Report AUPRC with confidence intervals computed by bootstrapping at the campaign level, not the host-day level. Additionally, report per-campaign precision-recall curves and AUPRC so the reader can see whether the aggregate number is driven by one dominant campaign. If the campaign-level bootstrap intervals for the GNN–GBDT AUPRC difference include zero, the result should be characterized as directional, not conclusive, regardless of the point estimate.

**Summary**: The methodology is more rigorous than most security ML papers. Issues 1 and 3 are the most consequential: Issue 1 could cause the wrong component of the system to receive credit for any observed gain, and Issue 3 could produce a statistically fragile conclusion that looks precise. Issue 2 is a correctness concern about label construction near split boundaries that could bias both models equally and should be audited rather than assumed away. None of these are fatal if addressed, but Issue 1 in particular requires a design change (adding a second GBDT trained on GNN node features) to make the ablation argument valid.
all_issues_raised: The edge-removal ablation cannot isolate relational inductive bias from richer cross-host feature content if GNN node features already encode multi-entity context that is absent from the tabular GBDT inputs.

The seven-day positive labeling window combined with campaign-grouping-by-first-observation may produce mislabeled host-days near split boundaries, specifically suppressing measured recall for early-campaign-stage detection.

AUPRC computed on a single contiguous three-month test window is dominated by a small number of campaigns, and bootstrap confidence intervals computed at the host-day level understate true estimator variance, making the GNN vs. GBDT comparison statistically fragile.

The seven-day positive labeling window and seven-day graph lookback window are co-extensive, so positive host-days after the malicious interval start have graphs that include live attack telemetry, creating a leakage asymmetry that specifically advantages the GNN over the GBDT.

Campaign containment by forensically determined first-observed date may misclassify early-phase host-days for slow-dwell intrusions whose scoping was completed after the training cutoff, introducing asymmetric label noise.

The edge-ablation control is applied only to the GNN, so it cannot distinguish whether GNN gains over GBDT come from learned graph structure or from joint optimization of node embeddings, because the GBDT already consumes hand-engineered relational features.

With a small number of distinct campaigns in the test split, AUPRC variance is dominated by campaign-specific telemetry similarity rather than architectural properties, making the confidence interval on the GNN-GBDT gap potentially too wide to support the stated conclusion.

The seven-day forward labeling window creates cross-boundary label leakage near split points that campaign containment does not fully prevent, inflating positive labels in late training from future-confirmed activity.

The ablation removing graph edges does not isolate relational structure from temporal path structure, because GNN message passing over temporally ordered edges encodes sequence information that the tabular baseline cannot access; a temporal-only baseline (e.g., LSTM over the event stream) is needed to separate these two sources of potential gain.

Hyperparameter tuning on months 12-15 implicitly adapts the architecture to campaign patterns in that period, which may resemble test-period campaigns more than future unseen campaigns, producing optimistic AUPRC estimates for the forward-looking generalization claim.

assessor_results.critic_raw:
The methodology is generally well-constructed. The time-based split, incident-campaign containment, preprocessing lockdown, and the edge-ablation control are all correct choices. However, there are several genuine methodological concerns worth raising, organized by root cause.

**1. The seven-day positive labeling window interacts asymmetrically with the graph's seven-day lookback window, creating a latent leakage path.**

The claim: host-days are labeled positive when they fall within seven days of a confirmed malicious interval, and the graph is constructed from the prior seven days of telemetry.

Why this might be wrong: Consider a host that is compromised on day D. Its host-day on day D-6 is labeled positive (within seven days of the malicious interval). The graph for that host-day on D-6 is built from telemetry spanning days D-13 through D-6. That is fine. But the host-day on day D+6 is *also* labeled positive (within seven days forward of a malicious interval start), and its graph is built from days D-1 through D+6 — which includes days D through D+6, i.e., active compromise telemetry. This means the GNN sees live attack signals as edge and node attributes when scoring a positive example, which is not a future-prediction task but a contemporaneous detection task. The labeling window and the graph lookback window have not been reconciled to ensure the model only sees pre-compromise telemetry for positive examples. The GBDT baseline is unaffected because it uses host-day aggregates that are presumably anchored to a single calendar day's features, so the asymmetry favors the GNN in a way that is not attributable to relational structure.

Evidence: Report the distribution of (label assignment day relative to malicious interval start) for positive host-days. If most positives are labeled because they fall *after* the start of the malicious interval rather than before it, and if the graph lookback captures that same period, the inflation of GNN AUPRC relative to GBDT will correlate with the fraction of positive host-days whose graph window overlaps confirmed malicious activity.

**2. Campaign containment by first observed malicious activity is correct in principle but depends on the quality of forensic scoping, which may systematically fail for slow intrusions.**

The claim: keeping all windows tied to the same incident or campaign in the same split based on first observed malicious activity prevents bleed.

Why this might be wrong: In enterprise IR, 'first observed malicious activity' is retrospectively determined from forensic artifacts. For slow-moving intrusions (living-off-the-land, long dwell-time APT), the forensically determined first-observed date is often weeks before the analyst actually generated the ticket and closed scoping. If the retrospective first-observed date falls in the training period but scoping was completed during the validation period, the campaign containment rule places all windows in training. That is correct. But the converse is also possible: a campaign whose first forensic artifact falls late in month 11 might have unresolved pre-cursor activity that bleeds into months 1-10, and the methodology excludes only unresolved host-days near open investigations — it does not exclude host-days that were resolved but whose forensic first-seen date was later revised. The result is that some training positives are correctly labeled and some training negatives are actually early-phase intrusion activity, depressing apparent class imbalance and adding noise that is not symmetric between model types.

Evidence: Check whether AUPRC on the validation set correlates with average dwell time of campaigns in that split. If longer-dwell campaigns are harder for both models equally, this is noise. If the GNN degrades more sharply on long-dwell campaigns (because its seven-day graph window does not capture slow behavioral drift), that would suggest the graph structure benefit is specific to fast-moving attacks and the general claim is overstated.

**3. The GBDT baseline's feature engineering partially replicates graph structure, so the edge-ablation control is the right test but is applied to the wrong model.**

The claim: the GBDT baseline with flattened relational features is the appropriate comparison, and an ablation removing graph edges while preserving node features tests whether GNN gains come from structure.

Why this might be wrong: The tabular features include lateral movement indicators, parent-child execution patterns, and authentication anomalies, which are themselves relational signals that have been pre-aggregated into scalar counts or statistics. The GBDT is not purely non-relational; it is consuming hand-engineered relational features. This means the comparison is not GNN-with-structure vs. no-structure, but GNN-with-learned-structure vs. GBDT-with-hand-engineered-structure. The edge ablation on the GNN tests whether the GNN's learned structure adds value over GNN node features alone, but it does not isolate whether the GNN's learned structure adds value over the GBDT's hand-engineered relational features. If the GNN wins by a small margin, it is unclear whether the win comes from better relational modeling or from the GNN jointly optimizing node embeddings and classification, which the GBDT cannot do. The correct additional control would be a GNN trained on node features only (the ablation) compared directly to the GBDT, not just the full GNN vs. GBDT.

Evidence: Report the ablated GNN (node features only, no edges) vs. GBDT AUPRC gap separately from the full GNN vs. GBDT gap. If the ablated GNN already matches or exceeds the GBDT, the residual gain from adding edges is the only defensible attribution to relational structure. If the ablated GNN underperforms the GBDT, the full GNN's win over GBDT could plausibly come from learned node representations rather than graph edges.

**4. Single-environment evaluation conflated with the stated generalization hypothesis.**

The claim: the experiment establishes whether graph-based relational inductive bias improves forward-looking intrusion detection.

Why this might be wrong: The methodology correctly disclaims transfer to other enterprises, but the internal generalization claim — that future campaigns are 'unseen' — depends on campaign novelty in the test period. If the 18-month window happens to contain only two or three large campaigns that dominate the positive class, then train/test splitting by time approximates splitting by campaign identity, and the test set may be effectively one or two held-out campaigns. In this regime, AUPRC variance is dominated by whether those specific campaigns happen to resemble training campaigns in telemetry signature, not by any property of the model architecture. A GNN win under these conditions may not replicate even on a 19th-month extension of the same environment.

Evidence: Report the number of distinct campaigns in each split, the positive host-day count per campaign, and the AUPRC estimate with bootstrap confidence intervals stratified by campaign. If the confidence interval on test AUPRC is wide enough that the GNN-GBDT gap falls within it, the conclusion cannot be drawn from this experiment alone.

In summary: issues 1 and 3 are the most structurally problematic. Issue 1 (label window / graph lookback asymmetry) is a potential leakage mechanism that specifically inflates GNN performance relative to GBDT. Issue 3 (edge ablation applied to wrong model) means the primary interpretive tool does not answer the question it is positioned to answer. Issues 2 and 4 reduce the strength of the generalization claim but do not invalidate the experimental design.

issues_raised: The seven-day forward labeling window creates cross-boundary label leakage near split points that campaign containment does not fully prevent, inflating positive labels in late training from future-confirmed activity.

The ablation removing graph edges does not isolate relational structure from temporal path structure, because GNN message passing over temporally ordered edges encodes sequence information that the tabular baseline cannot access; a temporal-only baseline (e.g., LSTM over the event stream) is needed to separate these two sources of potential gain.

Hyperparameter tuning on months 12-15 implicitly adapts the architecture to campaign patterns in that period, which may resemble test-period campaigns more than future unseen campaigns, producing optimistic AUPRC estimates for the forward-looking generalization claim.
assessor_results.critic_raw:
The methodology is unusually careful and most of the obvious traps are already addressed. The temporal split is correct, the campaign-level containment prevents intrusion-stage leakage, preprocessing is fit only on training, the ablation is designed to isolate structural benefit, and the scope is appropriately bounded. However, I find three issues that are genuine rather than manufactured.

**Issue 1: The seven-day positive labeling window creates asymmetric leakage between train and test that the campaign split does not fully repair.**

The claim: campaign containment based on 'first observed malicious activity' prevents future stages of an intrusion from appearing in training while earlier stages appear in test.

Why it might be wrong: The seven-day forward window means that a host-day on day D is labeled positive if any confirmed malicious activity falls within [D, D+7]. For a campaign whose first observed activity is near a split boundary — say, month 11 to 12 — the labeling window extends across the split. If first observed activity is assigned to validation (month 12), but some of the seven-day window covers late month 11 training data, those training host-days get a positive label derived from an incident 'known' only from the future. The campaign split prevents a host appearing in both train and test as the same campaign, but it does not prevent the label of a training host-day from being set by evidence that only became available later. The direction of leakage is subtle: the model never sees the validation activity directly, but the positive labels on adjacent training host-days are inflated by future-confirmed activity. This biases the model toward patterns that precede confirmed incidents, which is operationally desirable but methodologically means the training signal is partly constructed from ground truth that a real SOC wouldn't have at training time.

Evidence for or against: Recompute labels using only activity confirmed within the training window (i.e., incidents closed before month 12) and check whether class balance or AUPRC changes materially. If positive rate near the split boundary drops significantly, leakage was meaningful.

**Issue 2: The graph representation implicitly encodes temporal ordering through edge recency, which the tabular baseline cannot access, so the ablation does not cleanly isolate relational structure from temporal structure.**

The claim: the ablation 'removes graph edges while preserving node features' tests whether gain comes from structure rather than metadata alone.

Why it might be wrong: The heterogeneous graph is built from seven days of telemetry. Edge existence, edge type, and edge attributes naturally encode temporal co-occurrence and sequencing — e.g., process A spawning process B yesterday versus today, or a user authenticating to host X before host Y. The tabular baseline uses rolling behavioral deltas, which partially capture temporal change, but aggregated counts and deltas compress the sequence. When edges are removed from the GNN in the ablation, the node features that remain were computed with GNN-specific aggregation in mind and may already encode neighborhood summaries (depending on implementation). More importantly, the GNN with edges can propagate information along paths that are temporally ordered — this is structural *and* temporal information simultaneously. The baseline GBDT cannot access path-level temporal ordering even with identical raw features. So a GNN win could be attributable to temporal path reasoning rather than relational inductive bias per se. The ablation as described does not separate these two.

Evidence for or against: Add a sequential/temporal baseline — an LSTM or Transformer operating over the same seven-day event stream per host, without cross-host edges — and compare it against both the GBDT and the full GNN. If the temporal model matches the GNN, the gain is from temporal structure, not graph structure. If the GNN still wins, the relational claim is stronger.

**Issue 3: Validation-period hyperparameter tuning over months 12-15 risks implicit adaptation to campaigns active in that period, which may resemble test campaigns more than a truly held-out evaluation would.**

The claim: model selection on validation AUPRC with frozen choices, touching test once, constitutes a valid forward-looking evaluation.

Why it might be wrong: Months 12-15 and months 16-18 are drawn from the same 18-month enterprise environment. If the environment experienced two or three major campaign families over this period (typical for a single enterprise), the validation period likely contains one and the test period contains the next. Hyperparameter choices tuned on months 12-15 — particularly neighbor sampling depth, class weighting, and edge types — will implicitly favor architectures that work well on the campaign patterns present in that period. This is not a data leak in the traditional sense, but it is a form of distributional optimism: the architecture is selected for a validation distribution that may be more similar to test than either is to future campaigns in a new deployment. The experiment scope statement correctly says this doesn't prove transfer to other enterprises, but the issue is that it may not even prove transfer to month 19 in the same enterprise if that month brings a new campaign family not represented in months 12-15.

Evidence for or against: Perform a secondary evaluation using months 12-13 as validation and months 14-15 as a second held-out set, with models trained only on months 1-11. Compare AUPRC on months 14-15 to AUPRC on months 16-18. If there is a consistent gap, earlier validation-period campaigns are providing implicit signal about what architectures work, and the test AUPRC is optimistic about novel-campaign generalization.

**Summary:** Issues 1 and 3 are evaluation design concerns that could inflate reported performance. Issue 2 is a threat to the interpretive claim — the experiment may not establish that relational inductive bias specifically is responsible for any observed gain. None of these are fatal to running the experiment, but issue 2 in particular requires an additional baseline to make the central hypothesis testable rather than merely suggestive.
